metadata.generated_for: Post-YOLO detection print output — Flutter app
metadata.note: This is the definitive data object loaded after YOLO identifies a food. Drive all Module 2A UI from this file.
metadata.ojas_scale: 0–100. base_ojas_delta is the default change for this food. Audit answer adds bonus or penalty on top.

context_keys.hotel: User is eating at a restaurant, hotel, or canteen

ayurvedic_profile.virya: Thermal potency — Ushna (hot) or Sheeta (cold) or Sama (neutral)
ayurvedic_profile.guna: Ayurvedic physical qualities of the food
ayurvedic_profile.agni_impact: Effect on digestive fire — Deepana (kindles), Brimhana (nourishes/builds), Manda (suppresses)
ayurvedic_profile.ama_risk: Risk of creating undigested metabolic waste — low | moderate | high

nutritional_context.base_ojas_delta: Points added/subtracted to Ojas score for eating this food (before audit). Range: -10 to +15.
nutritional_context.best_meal_time: Ayurvedic optimal time to consume this food

deep_audit.home.ojas_bonus: Added to base_ojas_delta if user picks positive_label
deep_audit.home.ojas_penalty: Added to base_ojas_delta if user picks negative_label (negative number)
deep_audit.hotel.feedback_timer_required: If true, ask user to rate how they feel 30 min after eating

0.name: Appam

dosha_effect.summary: Pitta-Vata pacifying; Kapha neutral

ayurvedic_profile.agni_impact: Deepana — natural fermentation by Lactobacillus pre-digests starches, kindles Agni without over-stimulating

nutritional_context.best_meal_time: Breakfast or light dinner before 7 PM

avoid_seasons: Shishira (Winter: Jan–Feb) — Sheeta virya can weaken already suppressed Agni
ritucharya.reason: Cooling nature suits hot, humid seasons. In winter, pair with Ushna sides like hot Sambar or Coconut Stew to balance Sheeta virya.

prakriti_advice.vata: Excellent. The softness, moisture, and ideal pairing with coconut milk grounds Vata. Always eat warm, never cold.
prakriti_advice.pitta: Very good when naturally fermented. Sour batter or pairing with Kaara Chutney defeats the Pitta-pacifying benefit.
prakriti_advice.kapha: Acceptable in moderation — 2 Appams max. Snigdha guna can accumulate Kapha over daily use. Pair with light sides.

home.question: Was this Appam fermented naturally overnight (using coconut water or a small amount of previous batter), without commercial yeast, Eno, or baking soda?
home.positive_label: Yes — Natural Overnight Fermentation
home.negative_label: No — Used Yeast / Eno / Instant Batter
home.positive_reasoning: Natural fermentation by Lactobacillus bacteria converts complex starches into bioavailable short-chain fatty acids, reduces the glycemic index by 25–30%, and populates the gut with beneficial microbiome — this is Ayurvedic Agni enhancement (Deepana) happening at the molecular level. Live cultures survive cooking and colonize the gut lining, which Ayurveda identifies as the seat of Ojas production. This is the highest-quality Appam possible.
home.negative_reasoning: Commercial yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) and Eno (sodium bicarbonate) only produce CO2 for lift — zero probiotic benefit. Sodium from Eno shifts the food toward Lavana-Ushna properties, mildly aggravating Pitta. The batter remains unfermented, meaning raw starch reaches the large intestine undigested, producing Ama (endotoxins) — the primary enemy of Ojas in Ayurveda.

hotel.question: Are the Appam edges soft and lacy ivory-white, or crispy, browned, and charred at the rim?
hotel.positive_label: Soft, White, Lacy Edges — Properly Made
hotel.negative_label: Crispy, Browned, Charred Edges — Chemical Leavening
hotel.positive_reasoning: Soft, white Appam confirms no chemical leaveners, correct pan temperature, and genuine fermented batter. The porous sponge structure (Laghu guna) means it will digest within 2–3 hours, providing sustained Rasa (plasma nutrient) without overtaxing Agni.
hotel.negative_reasoning: Crispy brown edges mean baking soda was used and the pan was too hot. Sodium bicarbonate shifts pH, kills any probiotic potential, and high-temperature browning creates acrylamide — a Maillard product identified in research as Ama-forming. What should be a Sheeta-Laghu food now carries Ushna-Tikshna gunas, disrupting the Pitta-pacifying benefit entirely.
red_flags: Unnaturally pure white color (bleached rice flour)
Strong sour or vinegar-like smell (over-fermented batter — Pitta aggravating)
Very thick, dense base (maida/all-purpose flour added — increases Guru guna)
Wet or sticky texture (undercooked — produces Ama)

ideal_with: Thengai Chutney (28) — Sheeta + Sheeta = maximum Pitta pacification
Coconut milk stew — Snigdha complement
Paal (warm milk with jaggery)
avoid_with: Kaara Chutney (8) — Teekshna Pitta overload cancels all benefit
Boiled Egg (2) — heavy protein + light carb in same meal creates Ama

condition_warnings.advice: Naturally fermented Appam has significantly lower glycemic index — acceptable 1–2 per meal. Instant batter version should be avoided.
Natural fermentation helps IBS. Commercial yeast may worsen bloating and gas. Introduce slowly.
Avoid pairing with Kaara Chutney or Sambar (too acidic combined). Over-fermented batter directly worsens gastritis.

0.ayurvedic_wisdom: Appam prepared the traditional way is one of the rare foods that is simultaneously Deepana (kindles Agni), Laghu (light), and Ojas-building. The fermentation performed overnight is, in Ayurvedic language, a form of Samskara (purifying transformation) that makes the otherwise heavy rice flour into a nectar-like food.

1.name: Beetroot Poriyal

dosha_effect.summary: Pitta pacifying; Vata-Kapha neutral to beneficial

ayurvedic_profile.agni_impact: Mildly Deepana — mustard seeds and curry leaves in tadka kindle Agni; beetroot itself is neutral on Agni

nutritional_context.best_meal_time: Lunch (fat-soluble betacarotene absorption is best at midday)

avoid_seasons: Shishira (Winter) — Sheeta virya can suppress Agni; counter with more ghee and spice
ritucharya.reason: Cooling sweet nature makes it excellent in hot seasons. The iron content makes it particularly valuable in Varsha (monsoon) when immunity dips.

prakriti_advice.vata: Good with ghee tadka — the fat grounds the Laghu quality. Add a pinch of rock salt and ginger to balance Vata.
prakriti_advice.pitta: Excellent — one of the best side dishes for Pitta types. The cooling sweet taste directly pacifies Pitta.
prakriti_advice.kapha: Very good — Laghu, digestive spices in tadka, and low-fat preparation are ideal for Kapha management.

home.question: Was the Poriyal cooked with a ghee-based tadka containing mustard seeds, curry leaves, and fresh grated coconut added at the end?
home.positive_label: Yes — Ghee Tadka + Fresh Coconut
home.negative_label: No — Refined Oil or No Coconut
home.positive_reasoning: Ghee enables fat-soluble absorption of betacarotene from beetroot — bioavailability increases 6-7x compared to oil-free preparation. Mustard seeds (Sarshapa) + curry leaves create the classical Tridoshic Deepana combination per Charaka. Fresh grated coconut adds Snigdha-Sheeta guna that makes the dish Ojas-building. This is Ahara-Samyoga: specific ingredient combinations unlock therapeutic value greater than the sum of parts.
home.negative_reasoning: Refined oil (sunflower, palmolein) is processed with chemical solvents, has no Snigdha therapeutic quality, and cannot efficiently carry fat-soluble nutrients into the bloodstream. Without ghee, betacarotene absorption drops by 85%. Without coconut, the dish becomes Ruksha-Laghu — nutritious but not Ojas-building.

hotel.question: Is the Poriyal vibrant magenta-red with excessive oil, or is it a natural deep ruby-red with soft, distinct beetroot pieces?
hotel.positive_label: Natural Deep Ruby-Red, Light Oil, Distinct Pieces
hotel.negative_label: Vivid Magenta, Oily, or Watery/Mushy
hotel.positive_reasoning: Natural ruby-red color confirms fresh beetroot cooked correctly. Distinct pieces mean it was not pre-cooked and reheated. Light oil means Laghu guna is preserved — this will digest easily and contribute Rakta-Dhatu (blood tissue) nourishment.
hotel.negative_reasoning: Vivid magenta can indicate artificial food color added (common in commercial settings to make the dish more attractive). Watery or mushy texture means it was pre-cooked, refrigerated, and reheated — Purva Anna (stale food) is Tamasic, reduces Prana content, and produces Ama instead of nourishment.
red_flags: Uniformly bright magenta with no variation in color (artificial dye)
Excessive oil pooling in the container
Very mushy, no texture (reheated from previous day)
Presence of raw onion mixed in (viruddha with dairy if consumed with Paal)

ideal_with: White Rice (27) + Sambar (25) — the tridoshic classic plate
Nei Satham (16) for Vata types
Ragi Kali (9) for a Pitta-reducing power meal
avoid_with: Boiled Egg (2) in same meal — protein + iron-rich food combination can compete for absorption
Chicken 65 (4) — temperature and flavor clash

condition_warnings.advice: Beetroot is high in oxalates. Patients with calcium-oxalate kidney stones should limit or avoid.
Highly beneficial — pair with Vitamin C source (lemon) and ghee to maximize iron absorption.
Moderate glycemic index. Acceptable in small portions with ghee to slow glucose release.

1.ayurvedic_wisdom: Beetroot in Poriyal form is one of the best Rakta-Vardhaka (blood-building) foods available. The combination of its natural iron, the cooking transformation (Samskaara), and the Snigdha tadka makes it a subtle Rasayana — especially valuable for women after menstruation and for Pitta-dominant individuals who run 'hot'.

2.name: Boiled Egg

dosha_effect.summary: Vata pacifying; Pitta aggravating in excess; Kapha increasing

ayurvedic_profile.agni_impact: Brimhana — nourishing and building, but requires strong Agni (Guru guna); hard-boiled requires stronger Agni than soft-boiled

nutritional_context.best_meal_time: Breakfast or lunch only — never dinner (too Guru for weak evening Agni)

avoid_seasons: Grishma (Summer) — Ushna virya compounds summer heat, aggravates Pitta
ritucharya.reason: Strong winter Agni can digest the heavy protein. Summer already elevates Pitta — adding Ushna food pushes it toward inflammation, skin eruptions, and excess heat.

prakriti_advice.vata: Excellent grounding food. Soft-boiled with a pinch of rock salt and black pepper is ideal. Eat warm.
prakriti_advice.pitta: Limit to 1 per day. Hard-boiled with hot masala is a direct Pitta provocation. Prefer soft-boiled, plain.
prakriti_advice.kapha: 1 per day maximum. The Guru-Snigdha nature increases Kapha over time. Prefer eating with plenty of pepper.

home.question: Is this a free-range country egg (Naati Koli Muttai) with a deep orange yolk, and was it soft-boiled (set white, runny or jammy yolk)?
home.positive_label: Country Egg + Soft-Boiled
home.negative_label: Commercial Poultry Egg + Hard-Boiled
home.positive_reasoning: Country/free-range eggs have 3-6x higher omega-3 fatty acids, 25% less saturated fat, and significantly lower cortisol levels than battery-farmed eggs. The deep orange yolk indicates high lutein and zeaxanthin (eye Rasayana). Soft-boiling keeps proteins partially denatured — digestive enzymes can break them down 20% faster than fully denatured hard-boiled proteins. This is the difference between a Brimhana (building) food and an Ama-forming heavy food.
home.negative_reasoning: Commercial battery-farmed eggs are produced with antibiotics and growth hormones (Visha load), fed synthetic feed (no natural phytonutrients), and kept in stress conditions (high cortisol in the egg itself — Rajasic/Tamasic quality). Hard-boiling causes sulfur compounds in yolk to cross-link with iron, creating ferrous sulfide (the green ring) — Ayurveda identifies this as an incompatible compound formation, harder to digest and Ama-producing.

hotel.question: Is the egg served fresh and warm with a tender, soft texture, or cold, rubbery, and slathered in heavy spicy masala?
hotel.positive_label: Fresh, Warm, Plain or Light Seasoning
hotel.negative_label: Cold, Rubbery, or Drowning in Heavy Masala
hotel.positive_reasoning: A freshly boiled, warm egg served simply confirms it was prepared to order or very recently. The Ushna virya is intact and will support Agni. Simple seasoning (rock salt, pepper, lemon) is Deepana without creating Viruddha (incompatible combination).
hotel.negative_reasoning: Cold, rubbery egg signals it was boiled hours ago and refrigerated. Protein cross-linking in cold storage makes the protein matrix significantly harder to digest — this is Guru guna squared. Heavy masala (especially artificial colors, Ajinomoto) converts a simple protein into a Visha-laden, Ama-forming food. The Ushna egg + Ushna masala = excessive Pitta aggravation.
red_flags: Green-grey ring around yolk (severely overcooked — sulfur compound, direct Ama)
Very wet or rubbery white (undercooked or stored improperly)
Bright orange masala (artificial color — Tartrazine dye)
MSG / Ajinomoto in the masala (thirst and heaviness after eating)

ideal_with: Puthina Chutney (24) — digestive, cooling contrast
White Rice (27) + a little ghee for complete nutrition
Ragi Kali (9) — grounding complete meal
avoid_with: Paal Kolukattai (17) — Egg + Milk is classical Viruddha Ahara (incompatible proteins)
Thengai Chutney (28) — Egg + Milk-based: Viruddha
Dosa (6) with milk — fermented sour + egg creates Ama

condition_warnings.advice: Limit to 1 per day, prefer soft-boiled country egg. Egg yolk cholesterol impact is minimal for most people when Agni is strong.
Strictly soft-boiled, plain. Heavy masala preparation must be avoided.
High protein load. Consult Vaidya for appropriate portion.

2.ayurvedic_wisdom: The egg is Vata's best friend and Pitta's challenge. It is simultaneously one of the most building (Brimhana) and hardest to digest (Guru) animal foods. Charaka recommends eggs specifically for those doing heavy physical work (Vyayama) and for Vata disorders — context defines whether it is medicine or burden.

3.name: Carrot Poriyal

dosha_effect.summary: Tridoshic — balances all three doshas when prepared correctly

ayurvedic_profile.agni_impact: Deepana — aromatic volatile compounds in carrots + mustard tadka kindle Agni without aggravating any dosha

nutritional_context.best_meal_time: Lunch — fat-soluble betacarotene absorption peaks at midday with ghee

avoid_seasons: No avoidance — adjust spicing. More warming spices in Shishira.
ritucharya.reason: Tridoshic foods are seasonal-independent. Adjust accompanying spices to season: warming in winter, cooling combinations in summer.

prakriti_advice.vata: Excellent with ghee tadka. Add a small amount of grated coconut at the end. Eat warm.
prakriti_advice.pitta: Excellent. Do not use too many mustard seeds (Teekshna). Coconut addition makes it even better for Pitta.
prakriti_advice.kapha: Very good. Use minimal oil (just enough for tadka). Skip coconut addition if Kapha is high.

home.question: Was the Poriyal cooked with a ghee-based tadka (not refined oil) and eaten fresh while warm?
home.positive_label: Yes — Ghee Tadka, Fresh and Warm
home.negative_label: No — Refined Oil or Reheated
home.positive_reasoning: Betacarotene in carrots is a fat-soluble compound — it cannot be absorbed without dietary fat. Ghee specifically contains medium-chain triglycerides and butyric acid that transport betacarotene directly through the gut wall into the lymphatic system. Studies show 6-7x higher betacarotene bioavailability with fat vs without. This is the Ayurvedic Ahara-Samyoga principle: the right combination transforms ordinary food into Rasayana. Freshness preserves all volatile aromatic compounds (Prana).
home.negative_reasoning: Refined sunflower or palmolein oil is solvent-extracted at high heat, contains trace chemical residues, and lacks the therapeutic Snigdha quality of ghee. While betacarotene will still be partially absorbed, the Ojas-building pathway through ghee's butyric acid is lost. Reheated Poriyal loses all volatile aromatic compounds and has reduced Prana — Charaka calls stale food (Purva Anna) a direct cause of Ama accumulation.

hotel.question: Does the Poriyal taste naturally sweet with a slight crunch, or is it artificially sweet (sugar added) or overly oily?
hotel.positive_label: Naturally Sweet, Light Crunch, Fresh
hotel.negative_label: Artificially Sweet (Sugar), Overly Oily, or Mushy
hotel.positive_reasoning: Natural sweetness from carrots (fructose + glucose) with slight crunch means fresh carrots, correct cooking time, and no adulteration. This Laghu-Snigdha presentation maximizes easy digestion and Ojas production.
hotel.negative_reasoning: Sugar addition (common in Udupi-style restaurants) takes a Madhura-vipaka food and makes it excessively Madhura → Kapha increase, blood sugar spike, Ama formation in excess. Mushy texture means it was pre-cooked and reheated — Purva Anna, Tamasic quality.
red_flags: Unnaturally bright orange color (artificial color added)
Sugar granules visible
Very oily and glistening (poor oil absorption — wrong frying technique)
Mushy with no texture (pre-cooked, reheated)

ideal_with: Sambar (25) + White Rice (27) — the tridoshic power plate
Ven Pongal (30) for Vata types
Ragi Kali (9) for Pitta-Kapha types
avoid_with: Chicken 65 (4) — temperature and energy clash in same meal
Very sour preparations — Amla overload with Madhura vipaka food

condition_warnings.advice: Excellent food — betacarotene is protective for diabetic eye health. Raw carrot is lower glycemic than cooked, but cooked with ghee is more bioavailable.
Highly therapeutic — the single best food to improve this condition. Eat consistently with ghee.
Very good — low calorie, filling, Laghu guna. Minimize oil quantity.

3.ayurvedic_wisdom: Carrot Poriyal is rare in being truly Tridoshic — it does not favor one dosha at the expense of another. The combination of its natural sweetness (Madhura rasa), neutral thermal potency (Sama virya), and sweet post-digestive taste (Madhura vipaka) creates the trifecta that Ayurveda considers the hallmark of Rasayana foods. Simple, fresh, and cooked with ghee — this is medicine on a plate.

4.name: Chicken 65

dosha_effect.summary: Pitta-Vata strongly aggravating; Kapha aggravating (from oil)

ayurvedic_profile.agni_impact: Initially appears Deepana but is Manda — reused oil and heavy protein suppress Agni after initial stimulation. Creates Ama.

nutritional_context.best_meal_time: Lunch only if at all — strong Agni required. Never at dinner.

ideal_seasons: Shishira (Winter: Jan–Feb) — strong Agni may process it
avoid_seasons: Grishma (Summer)
Sharad (Autumn) — Pitta is already elevated; this pushes it into disorder
ritucharya.reason: Deep-fried, heavily spiced food requires maximum Agni. Winter is the only season where the body can reasonably manage this without Ama accumulation or Pitta eruption.

prakriti_advice.vata: Avoid — Ruksha (dry) coating with Ushna oil creates a contradictory Vata-aggravating combination. The dryness and heat dry out already depleted Vata.
prakriti_advice.pitta: Strongly avoid — every component (deep-fry, red chili, masala, reused oil) aggravates Pitta. Skin eruptions, gastritis, and anger are classical consequences.
prakriti_advice.kapha: Avoid — Guru + Snigdha (oil) increases Kapha. The stimulation feels satisfying to Kapha but creates cumulative damage.

home.question: Did you use fresh, single-use cold-pressed groundnut or sesame oil, and natural Kashmiri chili (not food dye) for the red color?
home.positive_label: Fresh Oil + Natural Kashmiri Chili Color
home.negative_label: Reused Oil or Artificial Food Dye
home.positive_reasoning: Fresh cold-pressed oil has no oxidized lipids. At correct frying temperature (170-175°C), a quick crust forms reducing total oil absorption. Kashmiri chili gives color through carotenoids (Capsanthin), not artificial dye — these carotenoids are antioxidant and anti-inflammatory. Even so, this is still a high-Ojas-reducing food; the fresh preparation only limits the damage.
home.negative_reasoning: Reused oil is the single biggest Ojas destroyer in cooked food. Each heating cycle creates trans fats, oxidized fatty acids (aldehydes, acrolein), and lipid peroxides — collectively identified as Visha (subtle toxins) in Ayurveda. Tartrazine and Allura Red (artificial dyes used for the characteristic bright red) are processed by the liver as foreign chemicals, creating Pitta aggravation in the Rakta (blood) and Rasa (plasma) dhatus. This pushes the base penalty from -5 to -13.

hotel.question: Is the chicken unnaturally bright red (food dye) and does it leave a strong artificial flavor aftertaste?
hotel.positive_label: Natural Brownish-Red, Clean Chicken Flavor
hotel.negative_label: Neon Red, Artificial Aftertaste, Heavy MSG
hotel.positive_reasoning: Natural brownish-red from Kashmiri chili and cooking means at least some care was taken with ingredients. The chicken flavor should be identifiable underneath the spice. This limits the Ojas damage to the baseline of deep-frying.
hotel.negative_reasoning: Neon red = Tartrazine/Allura Red artificial dye. These are Pitta-Rakta toxins that cause hyperactivity, allergic reactions, and liver stress. Ajinomoto (MSG) creates excessive thirst, inflammation, and over-stimulates the nervous system (Vata-Pitta aggravation). Restaurant Chicken 65 typically uses oil that has been heated 5–15+ times — each cycle multiplying the Visha load exponentially.
red_flags: Neon/fluorescent red color that stains fingers
Strong artificial aftertaste (MSG, artificial flavor)
Very greasy or leaves orange oil stain on paper
Extremely hard and dry texture (over-fried in low-quality reused oil)
Excessive thirst 20–30 minutes after eating (Ajinomoto signature)

ideal_with: If consumed: Puthina Chutney (24) — the cooling, Pitta-pacifying chutney partially offsets the damage
Fresh lime juice — Vitamin C and cooling contrast
avoid_with: Paal Kolukattai (17) — Chicken + Milk: critical Viruddha Ahara
Any dairy in the same meal
Alcohol — multiplies Pitta aggravation and Visha load significantly

condition_warnings.advice: Absolute contraindication. Will cause immediate aggravation.
Direct Pitta-Rakta aggravation. Must avoid completely.
High sodium, MSG, and inflammatory load — contraindicated.
Reused oil toxins, artificial colors, and MSG are particularly harmful. Avoid.

4.ayurvedic_wisdom: Chicken 65 represents Tamasic food at its most concentrated — the combination of reused oil (Dushta Sneha), artificial color (Visha), excessive spice (Teekshna), and factory-farmed meat (Rajasic quality). Even in its best home-made version with fresh ingredients, it is a Pitta-Vata aggravating food. Eat it as an occasional indulgence, never as a regular meal. If your body shows signs (heartburn, skin eruptions, anger, thirst) — it is your Pitta communicating clearly.

5.name: Chicken Biryani

dosha_effect.summary: Vata-Pitta aggravating; Kapha neutral to aggravating

ayurvedic_profile.agni_impact: Whole spices are Deepana but the overall heavy fat + protein combination is Manda for post-meal digestion. Creates digestive load.

nutritional_context.best_meal_time: Lunch only — strong midday Agni can handle it. Never dinner.

avoid_seasons: Grishma (Summer)
Sharad (Autumn) — Pitta aggravation
ritucharya.reason: Heavy rice and meat combination requires the body's strongest digestive capacity. Winter maximizes Agni. Summer and autumn Pitta seasons make this a recipe for inflammation and indigestion.

prakriti_advice.vata: Acceptable IF made with ghee and proper whole spices. The heavy, warm, unctuous nature grounds Vata. However, leftover/reheated biryani is strongly Vata-aggravating.
prakriti_advice.pitta: Limit consumption to 1–2 times per month. Ensure no Ajinomoto, minimal chili, and eat with cooling Raita.
prakriti_advice.kapha: Avoid or eat very small portions. Heavy combination of rice + meat + oil is too Guru-Snigdha for Kapha types.

home.question: Is it made with pure cow ghee (not Dalda or refined oil), whole spices (not masala powder), and quality Seeraga Samba or aged Basmati rice?
home.positive_label: Pure Ghee + Whole Spices + Quality Rice
home.negative_label: Dalda/Oil + Masala Powder + Low-quality Rice
home.positive_reasoning: Cow ghee is Charaka's #1 Ojas-building fat — it is the only fat that transforms a heavy meal into a partially nourishing one by carrying fat-soluble nutrients, coating the gut lining, and supporting Agni through its butyric acid content. Whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf) are Deepana, reduce the Guru burden of the meal, and their volatile oils remain intact. Seeraga Samba or aged Basmati have lower starch density than regular rice — glycemic impact is moderated.
home.negative_reasoning: Dalda (Vanaspati / hydrogenated vegetable fat) contains up to 40% trans fats — the most directly Ojas-destroying cooking medium. Trans fats interfere with cell membrane function, trigger systemic inflammation (Shonita dushana — blood vitiation), and have zero nutritional value. Masala powder is typically old, has oxidized volatile oils, excess salt, and often contains anti-caking agents. The combination of Dalda + powder masala = maximum Ama-forming biryani.

hotel.question: Do you feel heavy, very thirsty, or notice a strange aftertaste/headache 30 minutes after eating the biryani?
hotel.positive_label: Feel Light — Normal Satiety, No Unusual Thirst
hotel.negative_label: Feel Heavy, Thirsty, or Notice MSG Symptoms
hotel.positive_reasoning: Feeling light and normally satisfied means the biryani was cooked with real ingredients, appropriate oil, and no MSG. The body's Agni is processing it without distress. Normal satiety (not excessive) means portion was appropriate.
hotel.negative_reasoning: Excessive thirst 30 minutes after eating is the signature of Ajinomoto (MSG) — it causes intracellular dehydration by altering sodium-potassium cellular balance. Heaviness indicates excess Dalda/cheap oil. Headache indicates histamine from either poor-quality chicken or artificial additives. These are all symptoms of Ama formation and Visha accumulation — direct Ojas depletion.
red_flags: Excessive thirst 20–30 minutes after eating (MSG)
Unnaturally yellow rice (artificial color)
Strong artificial masala smell
Oil pooling at bottom of container
Very heavy, bloated feeling 1 hour post-meal

ideal_with: Puthina Chutney (24) — classic pairing, cooling offset for Pitta
Plain Raita (curd + cumin) — probiotics help with heavy protein digestion
avoid_with: Paal Kolukattai (17) — Chicken + Milk: critical Viruddha Ahara
Cold drinks or ice water — suppresses Agni when it's needed most to process this meal
Medu Vadai (12) — double deep-fried heavy load in one meal

condition_warnings.advice: Avoid restaurant biryani. Home version with ghee and minimal chili may be acceptable once a week.
High Vata-aggravating food. Likely to trigger flare-ups, especially with MSG.
Restaurant versions are very high sodium (salt + MSG + pickle). Contraindicated.

5.ayurvedic_wisdom: Chicken Biryani sits at the intersection of Rajasic and Tamasic food. Made at home with ghee and whole spices, it is heavy but nourishing — an occasional feast food. From a restaurant with Dalda, MSG, and artificial color, it becomes a concentrated dose of what Ayurveda calls Visha — subtle toxins that accumulate silently in the Dhatus (body tissues) and deplete Ojas over repeated consumption.

6.name: Dosa

dosha_effect.summary: Pitta increasing when sour; Vata-Kapha beneficial when fresh

ayurvedic_profile.agni_impact: Deepana when batter is fresh (active fermentation metabolites). Ama-forming when batter is old (excess acid, degraded proteins).

nutritional_context.best_meal_time: Breakfast — ideally before 8 AM when Agni is building
nutritional_context.digestibility: easy when fresh; moderate when sour

ideal_seasons: Hemanta — the Ushna quality is welcome in cold seasons
avoid_seasons: Sharad (Autumn) — Pitta is already at peak; sour fermented food escalates it
ritucharya.reason: Dosa's Amla vipaka naturally increases Pitta. In Pitta-dominant seasons (Grishma, Sharad), daily dosa creates cumulative Pitta aggravation. Once or twice per week is reasonable year-round; daily consumption in summer is inadvisable for Pitta types.

prakriti_advice.vata: Good — the warm, thin, slightly sour crepe with oil is grounding. Prefer Set Dosa (thicker, softer) over Paper Roast.
prakriti_advice.pitta: Moderate — fresh batter, no over-fermentation. Pair with cooling Thengai Chutney. Avoid red chutneys. Limit to once daily.
prakriti_advice.kapha: Excellent — Laghu, Ruksha (dry) nature of crispy dosa is good for Kapha. Can eat Paper Roast version.

home.question: Is the batter freshly fermented (1–2 days maximum) and cooked on a well-seasoned iron or cast-iron tawa — not a non-stick coated pan?
home.positive_label: Fresh Batter (1–2 days) + Iron Tawa
home.negative_label: Old/Over-Sour Batter or Non-stick Pan
home.positive_reasoning: 1–2 day fermented batter contains active Lactobacillus that produce the optimal lactic acid ratio — mildly Amla (healthy sourness) without tipping into Atyamla (excessive sour). The batter is still pre-digesting proteins and reducing phytates. An iron tawa transfers bioavailable dietary iron into the food — this is Ayurvedic Loha Rasayana (iron as therapeutic agent). The cast iron also retains and distributes heat evenly, creating perfect Laghu texture.
home.negative_reasoning: Old batter (3+ days) develops excess lactic and acetic acid, degraded protein, and begins producing ethanol. This extreme Amla quality directly aggravates Pitta — Charaka lists excessive sour taste as a direct cause of Rakta dushana (blood vitiation), skin eruptions, and inflammation. Non-stick (Teflon) pans at high heat release PTFE fumes and have no iron transfer benefit.

hotel.question: Is it a thin-crispy Paper Roast drenched in oil, or a softer Set Dosa / plain Dosa with minimal oil?
hotel.positive_label: Set/Soft Dosa, Minimal Oil
hotel.negative_label: Paper Roast Dosa, Drenched in Oil
hotel.positive_reasoning: A soft, thin dosa with minimal oil means correct tawa temperature, good batter hydration, and reasonable oil use. This maximizes Laghu guna — it will digest in 2–3 hours and supply steady Rasa (plasma nutrient) without taxing Agni.
hotel.negative_reasoning: Paper Roast Dosa requires excessive oil and very high heat — this increases Ruksha + Ushna + Teekshna gunas simultaneously. The charred edges create acrolein and Maillard byproducts. The excessive oil creates a Snigdha-Ushna contradiction that first stimulates and then suppresses Agni — classic Ama-formation mechanism. Restaurant Paper Roast often uses low-quality refined oil in large quantities.
red_flags: Very dark brown or charred edges (burned — acrolein formation)
Oil pooling on the plate after the dosa is removed
Very sour smell (over-fermented, spoiled batter)
Unnaturally white or uniform texture (maida added to batter)

ideal_with: Thengai Chutney (28) — cooling Sheeta offsets Dosa's Ushna
Sambar (25) — the perfect Deepana complement
Puthina Chutney (24) — Pitta-pacifying cooling option
avoid_with: Paal Kolukattai (17) — sour fermented + milk = Viruddha (Ama formation)
Chicken 65 (4) — Pitta + Pitta double aggravation
Cold milk or cold drinks immediately after — shock to Agni

condition_warnings.advice: Fresh batter only, soft dosa, with cooling chutneys. Over-sour batter is an immediate trigger.
Limit to once per week. The Amla vipaka accumulates Pitta in Rakta dhatu.
Rice-lentil batter is goitrogen-free. This is fine. However, avoid pairing with raw cruciferous vegetables.

6.ayurvedic_wisdom: Dosa is Ayurveda's gift to the working person — a quick, light, fermented food that is satisfying without being heavy. Its secret is in the batter. Fresh batter is medicine; old batter is a Pitta-poison. The iron tawa is not a cooking detail — it is a nutritional delivery mechanism. A properly made Dosa with Thengai Chutney and Sambar is a complete, balanced Ayurvedic meal.

7.name: Idly
7.classification: Steamed Fermented Rice-Lentil Cake — Supreme Sattvic Food

dosha_effect.summary: Kapha-Pitta balancing; Vata neutral to pacifying

ayurvedic_profile.agni_impact: Deepana-Brimhana: Simultaneously kindles Agni (fermentation metabolites) AND nourishes body tissues. This dual action is extremely rare and marks it as a therapeutic food.
ayurvedic_profile.ama_risk: low (lowest Ama risk of all grain preparations)

nutritional_context.best_meal_time: Breakfast — the ideal start to the day. Suits all seasons.

ideal_seasons: All seasons — the only truly all-season South Indian staple food
avoid_seasons: No season-based avoidance. In Shishira (winter), pair with hot Sambar and Ghee to compensate for cooling nature.
ritucharya.reason: Steam-cooked, naturally fermented, and light — Idly adapts to every season with appropriate accompaniments. This seasonal adaptability is itself a mark of Tridoshic balance.

prakriti_advice.vata: Good — eat warm with ghee and Sambar. The warmth and Snigdha accompaniment ground Vata. Avoid eating cold or with cold chutneys.
prakriti_advice.pitta: Excellent — steam cooking is the most Sattvic method. Sheeta virya directly pacifies Pitta. Pair with cooling Thengai Chutney.
prakriti_advice.kapha: Very good — Laghu and Mridu. Avoid excess oil (no soaking in oil or excessive Ghee). Prefer with spicy Sambar to keep Kapha moving.

home.question: Was the batter fermented naturally overnight (8–12 hours, without Eno or yeast) in a warm place, and are the Idlys steamed fresh and served immediately?
home.positive_label: Natural Overnight Fermentation + Fresh Steamed
home.negative_label: Instant Batter (Eno/Yeast) or Reheated Idlys
home.positive_reasoning: Natural overnight fermentation by Lactobacillus mesenteroides and Leuconostoc species is one of the most well-researched probiotic processes in traditional food. It produces B12 (rare in plant food), GABA (calming neurotransmitter), short-chain fatty acids, and reduces phytic acid by 90% — dramatically increasing iron, calcium, and zinc bioavailability. Steam cooking (Svedana in Ayurveda) is the most gentle, Sattvic cooking method — it preserves Prana, adds no fat, and creates Laghu quality from heavy rice. Eating fresh means maximum probiotic count and Prana content. This is Idly at its maximum therapeutic value — the equivalent of a health supplement in food form.
home.negative_reasoning: Eno or yeast-based instant batter produces CO2 only — no probiotic cultures, no B12, no GABA. Sodium from Eno adds Lavana (salt) quality, mildly aggravating Pitta. Reheated Idlys undergo retrogradation (starch crystallization) — the texture becomes rubbery and the starch changes from easily digestible to resistant starch that reaches the large intestine undigested → Ama formation. Microwave reheating additionally alters the electrical polarity of water molecules in the Idly — Ayurveda identifies this as Tejas-depleting.

hotel.question: Is the Idly soft, slightly spongy, and naturally off-white, or rubbery, extremely white, and dense?
hotel.positive_label: Soft, Spongy, Natural Off-White Color
hotel.negative_label: Rubbery, Dense, or Unnaturally Pure White
hotel.positive_reasoning: Soft, spongy, off-white Idly confirms naturally fermented batter at correct ratio (3:1 rice:urad dal), proper fermentation time, and correct steaming duration (10–12 minutes). Spongy texture means the starch gelatinization happened optimally — maximum digestibility, Laghu quality achieved. Natural off-white color means no bleaching agents.
hotel.negative_reasoning: Rubbery texture = over-steamed OR baking soda used excessively. Excessively white = bleached rice flour or high proportion of rice flour vs urad dal (cheating the ratio). Dense, non-spongy = batter was not fermented, just rice flour with water — zero probiotic benefit, maximum starch load, pure Kapha-increasing Guru food with no Laghu compensation.
red_flags: Unnaturally pure white color (bleached rice flour)
Rubbery, chewy texture (over-steaming or baking soda excess)
Flat, dense, not spongy (insufficient fermentation)
Sticky or wet inside (understeamed — raw starch, maximum Ama risk)

ideal_with: Sambar (25) — the perfect Ojas pairing — complete protein, Deepana spices
Thengai Chutney (28) — cooling Pitta-pacifying complement
A small amount of pure ghee — Brimhana upgrade, especially for Vata and Pitta
avoid_with: Dosa batter that is over-fermented (sour batter makes sour Idly — Pitta aggravation)
Cold milk immediately after — thermal shock to Agni
Egg (2) in same meal — heavy protein + light carb creates Ama for most Prakriti types

condition_warnings.condition: All conditions — Idly is safe for nearly everyone
condition_warnings.advice: This is one of the few foods that is broadly safe across most health conditions when naturally fermented.
Excellent — naturally gluten-free. One of the best grain preparations for gluten-sensitive individuals.
Naturally fermented Idly has significantly reduced glycemic index. 2–3 Idlys with Sambar (not coconut chutney) is an excellent diabetic-friendly breakfast.

7.ayurvedic_wisdom: Idly is the crown jewel of South Indian Ayurvedic cooking. Charaka Samhita describes steamed grain preparations (Svedana Anna) as the most Sattvic of all cooked foods. The natural fermentation process is described in Bhavaprakasha as a form of Agni outside the body — pre-digesting, purifying, and enhancing the grain's rasayana properties. If you could eat only one food every day and maintain health — properly made, freshly steamed Idly with Sambar would be the Ayurvedic recommendation.

8.name: Kaara Chutney
8.classification: Spiced Red Chili Chutney — Pitta Aggravating

dosha_effect.summary: Pitta strongly aggravating; Kapha pacifying (clears mucus); Vata aggravating in excess

ayurvedic_profile.agni_impact: Initially strongly Deepana — stimulates Agni rapidly. However excess or daily use leads to Tivra Agni (hyperactive Agni) which damages the gut lining — Pitta disorder.
ayurvedic_profile.ama_risk: low (itself Ama-clearing) but creates Pitta disorders with excess use

nutritional_context.best_meal_time: Small quantity at breakfast or lunch only

ideal_seasons: Varsha (Monsoon — when Agni is weak and needs stimulation)
avoid_seasons: Grishma (Summer)
Sharad (Autumn) — Pitta seasons; this is adding fuel to fire literally
ritucharya.reason: Kaara Chutney's job is to kindle Agni — in winter and monsoon this is valuable. In summer and autumn, Pitta is already elevated and needs cooling food, not Teekshna stimulation.

prakriti_advice.vata: Use sparingly — very small amount with ghee-rich food. The Teekshna-Ushna quality dries Vata further. One tablespoon maximum.
prakriti_advice.pitta: Strongly minimize. If Pitta is already imbalanced (heartburn, skin issues, anger), eliminate entirely. If Pitta is in balance, a tiny amount 2–3 times per week is acceptable.
prakriti_advice.kapha: Beneficial in appropriate amounts — clears Kapha from channels, stimulates Agni, reduces mucus. Kapha can tolerate this best of all three types.

home.question: Did you temper it with Gingelly (sesame) oil and add a small amount of tamarind to balance the heat with a light sour note?
home.positive_label: Yes — Gingelly Oil Tadka + Tamarind Balance
home.negative_label: No — Dry, No Balancing Ingredient
home.positive_reasoning: Gingelly (sesame) oil is Sheeta in long-term effect despite being Ushna in immediate action — it specifically counters the long-term Pitta aggravation of red chili. Charaka recommends sesame oil as the best oil for Pitta-Vata pacification. The tiny tamarind adds Amla rasa which acts as Anupana (vehicle) that distributes and moderates the chili's Teekshna quality across the gut without concentrating it.
home.negative_reasoning: Without balancing ingredients, pure red chili chutney is all Teekshna-Ushna-Katu — directly attacking the stomach lining (Amashaya), producing acute Pitta in the gut. Daily use without sesame oil balance leads to hyperacidity, inflammation, and eventual Rakta-Pitta (bleeding disorders) per Ayurvedic texts.

hotel.question: Does eating it cause an immediate burning sensation in the mouth or throat, or is the heat moderate and dissipating?
hotel.positive_label: Moderate Heat, Pungent but Manageable
hotel.negative_label: Immediate Burning, Throat Irritation
hotel.positive_reasoning: Moderate heat from quality red chilies creates Deepana without tissue damage. The heat should peak and dissipate — indicating fresh chilies and appropriate quantity.
hotel.negative_reasoning: Immediate burning sensation indicates either: (1) excessive quantity of low-quality dried chilies (Teekshna++ concentration), or (2) addition of spice extracts/oleoresins to increase pungency cheaply. Both bypass the normal Deepana pathway and directly damage the stomach and throat mucosa — Pitta aggravation at first contact.
red_flags: Bright red (artificial color mixed into chutney)
Burning sensation that lasts more than 10 minutes in throat
Extremely oily with cheap refined oil floating on top
Very salty (excess sodium to mask low-quality chilies)

ideal_with: Idly (7) — 1 tablespoon max; the mild Idly absorbs and moderates the chutney's intensity
Medu Vadai (12) — small amount to counter Kapha from fried food
avoid_with: Any milk or dairy in same meal — Teekshna + Sheeta-Guru = direct Viruddha
Already spicy dishes — Pitta stacking
Appam (0) — defeats the Pitta-pacifying benefit of Appam entirely

condition_warnings.advice: Absolute contraindication — even a small amount will trigger immediate aggravation.
Avoid entirely. Teekshna quality directly aggravates Rakta-Pitta through skin channels.
Contraindicated — this is Teekshna attacking already inflamed tissue.

8.ayurvedic_wisdom: Kaara Chutney is a medicine in small amounts and a poison in excess — the classical Ayurvedic definition of Matra (dose) determining efficacy vs toxicity. Used wisely, one tablespoon with your Idly wakes up a sluggish Agni and clears Kapha from the respiratory tract. Used foolishly as a primary flavoring across every meal, it burns through the gut lining and creates the very diseases it was meant to prevent.

9.name: Kali (Ragi / Wheat Kali)
9.classification: Finger Millet or Wheat Porridge — Dense Sattvic Grain

dosha_effect.summary: Kapha increasing in excess; Vata-Pitta pacifying in appropriate portions

ayurvedic_profile.agni_impact: Brimhana (Building-Nourishing) — strengthens body tissues, especially Asthi (bone) and Mamsa (muscle). Requires strong Agni to digest fully.
ayurvedic_profile.ama_risk: low to moderate (depends on Agni strength)

nutritional_context.best_meal_time: Lunch or early dinner (before 6 PM) — heavy grain needs full digestive window

avoid_seasons: Shishira (Winter) — Sheeta virya + Kapha season = Kapha overload. If eaten in winter, pair with warming spices (pepper, ginger).
ritucharya.reason: Ragi Kali's cooling, heavy nature is perfect for summer and autumn. In winter, its Sheeta virya can dampen Agni — counter with warming companions.

prakriti_advice.vata: Excellent — Guru, Sthira, Snigdha qualities perfectly ground unstable Vata. Add ghee liberally. This is one of the best Vata-balancing grains.
prakriti_advice.pitta: Very good — Sheeta virya directly cools Pitta. Ragi's calcium content is highest of all grains — bones (Asthi dhatu) governed by Vata, strengthened by this food.
prakriti_advice.kapha: Limit portions. Guru-Snigdha combination increases Kapha. Eat with plenty of pepper and ginger to counter. Wheat Kali is heavier than Ragi — prefer Ragi.

home.question: Is it made from freshly milled or recently purchased sprouted whole-grain Ragi flour (dark brownish color, strong earthy aroma)?
home.positive_label: Sprouted Whole-Grain Ragi, Fresh Flour
home.negative_label: Store-bought Refined / Old Ragi Flour
home.positive_reasoning: Sprouting Ragi before milling reduces phytic acid by 40–60%, dramatically increasing bioavailability of Ragi's exceptional mineral content (highest calcium of any grain — 344mg/100g vs 10mg in rice). Sprouting also activates Amylase enzymes that pre-digest starches — making Guru grain significantly easier on Agni. Fresh flour retains all volatile aromatic compounds (Prana). This is Samskara (transformation) at work — the ancient practice of sprouting before milling is Ayurvedic medicine, not food processing.
home.negative_reasoning: Old store-bought flour has undergone lipid oxidation (rancidity of the germ fats) — introducing Dushta Sneha (degraded fat) into what should be a pure grain. Refined flour has had the bran removed — losing the fiber, calcium, and iron that make Ragi a Rasayana. Stale flour also lacks Prana — it nourishes the physical body but not the subtle Ojas.

hotel.question: Is the Kali heavy and oily with lots of added fat, or is it firm, dry-outside with a small amount of ghee?
hotel.positive_label: Firm, Lightly Finished with Ghee
hotel.negative_label: Very Oily, Excessive Fat Added
hotel.positive_reasoning: Properly made Ragi Kali should be firm, hold its shape (Sthira guna), and have a natural earthy aroma. A tablespoon of ghee for serving is ideal — Brimhana without excessive Guru.
hotel.negative_reasoning: Excess oil (especially cheap refined oils or Dalda) in Kali converts a therapeutic grain preparation into an Ama-forming heavy food. The Guru guna of Ragi multiplied by excess Snigdha fat = maximum digestive load. Commercial hotels sometimes add Dalda to improve texture cheaply.
red_flags: Waxy texture or artificial sheen (Dalda added)
Very soft, not holding shape (water quality issue or maida added to dilute)
No earthy Ragi aroma (old flour or heavily refined)

ideal_with: Sambar (25) — Deepana spices complement Ragi's Brimhana quality
Drumstick (Murungai) accompaniment — calcium synergy
Buttermilk with cumin — Agni support for heavy grain
avoid_with: Cold milk or cold foods immediately after — thermal conflict
Sweet preparations simultaneously — Madhura overload increases Kapha rapidly

condition_warnings.advice: Highly therapeutic — highest calcium content of any grain. Eat daily with ghee for maximum benefit.
Excellent — very low glycemic index (54 vs rice at 72). The fiber slows glucose release. Preferred grain for diabetics.
Ragi itself is gluten-free. However, cross-contamination with wheat during milling is common — ensure certified GF source.

9.ayurvedic_wisdom: Ragi Kali is Karnataka's original Rasayana — a fact modern nutritional science is now confirming. The ancient practice of sprouting before cooking, the calcium density, the Brimhana quality that builds Asthi dhatu (bone tissue) — this is not folk food, it is sophisticated Ayurvedic pharmacology encoded as a staple meal. Charaka places grain preparations that are Guru-Snigdha-Sthira at the top of foods that build Ojas, with the caveat that Agni must be respected.

10.name: Koozh (Millet Porridge)
10.classification: Fermented Millet Porridge — Ancient Tamil Probiotic Superfood

dosha_effect.summary: Pitta-Vata balancing; Kapha neutral (despite cooling nature, the Deepana quality prevents Kapha accumulation)

ayurvedic_profile.agni_impact: Deepana + Brimhana simultaneously — the rarest combination in food. Fermentation-Deepana + grain-Brimhana = rebuilds Agni while nourishing all Dhatus.

nutritional_context.best_meal_time: Morning or mid-morning — serves as primary nourishment for manual workers; excellent pre-noon meal

ideal_seasons: Varsha (Monsoon) — probiotic boost when immunity dips
avoid_seasons: Shishira (Winter) — Sheeta virya can suppress already-weakened winter Agni. Pair with warming sides if consumed.
ritucharya.reason: Koozh is Tamil Nadu's answer to summer heat — its Sheeta virya and probiotic content make it the ultimate cooling, gut-healing food for hot seasons. The fact that it was the traditional food of farmers doing intense labor in summer heat confirms its Brimhana-Sheeta dual action.

prakriti_advice.vata: Good — the Snigdha-Mridu quality moderates Vata. Add a pinch of rock salt and a small amount of sesame oil. Eat at room temperature, not cold.
prakriti_advice.pitta: Excellent — one of the best foods for Pitta. The cooling fermented millet directly pacifies Pitta in the Rakta and Rasa dhatus. Can eat cold from fermentation pot.
prakriti_advice.kapha: Good — Laghu quality prevents Kapha increase despite appearing dense. Avoid adding excess salt. Eat with small raw onions (Deepana for Kapha).

home.question: Was it fermented overnight in a mud pot (not steel, not plastic), and is it served cold/room temperature directly from the pot with raw pearl onions and salt?
home.positive_label: Mud Pot Fermented + Served at Correct Temperature
home.negative_label: Steel/Plastic Container or Reheated
home.positive_reasoning: The mud pot (Matti Paanai) is arguably as important as the ingredients. Clay's mineral-rich, porous surface harbors specific traditional microbiome strains that produce Koozh's unique flavor compounds and therapeutic Lactobacillus species. Clay also acts as a natural coolant, maintaining ideal fermentation temperature (25–30°C). Clay adds trace minerals (silica, iron, calcium) to the ferment. Raw pearl onions are classical Deepana — raw onion (Palaandu) is Katu-Ushna, stimulating Agni and completing the thermally balanced meal (Sheeta Koozh + Ushna onion = perfect balance).
home.negative_reasoning: Steel is inert — loses all mineral contribution and maintains temperature differently, potentially disrupting optimal fermentation strains. Plastic may leach BPA/phthalates into the acidic fermented porridge — direct Visha introduction. Reheating Koozh destroys the live Lactobacillus cultures (die at 42°C+), converting a probiotic-rich food into just plain cooked millet porridge — losing its primary therapeutic value and converting Deepana-Brimhana to merely Brimhana.

hotel.question: Is it served cold/room temperature with traditional accompaniments (raw onion, green chili, salt), or is it thick, reheated, and heavily salted?
hotel.positive_label: Traditional Presentation — Cold, Raw Onion, Appropriate Consistency
hotel.negative_label: Reheated, Thick, Heavily Salted or Sweet
hotel.positive_reasoning: Traditional Koozh should be pourable — like a thick lassi. Cold/room temperature means live cultures intact. Raw onion accompaniment means the hotel understands the traditional preparation. This is therapeutic food being served correctly.
hotel.negative_reasoning: Reheated = no probiotics, just starchy millet porridge. Overly thick = too much grain, too little water, poor fermentation ratio. Heavy salt = kidneys stressed. Sweet Koozh (some restaurants add sugar) converts a Sheeta-Deepana food into a pure Madhura-Kapha-increasing preparation — defeating the entire therapeutic purpose.
red_flags: Served hot or steaming (all probiotics destroyed)
Unnaturally thick or paste-like (wrong ratio, poor fermentation)
Very salty or sweetened
Bland taste (insufficient fermentation — no lactic acid profile)

ideal_with: Raw pearl onions (Chinna Vengayam) — Deepana, Pitta-balancing
Green chili (small) — Agni stimulus
Pickle (Urugai) in tiny quantity — probiotic companion
avoid_with: Hot beverages immediately after — destroys live cultures
Dairy — fermented millet + milk creates Ama (Viruddha Ahara)
Sugar additions — converts Deepana food to pure Kapha-increasing sweet

condition_warnings.advice: The #1 food for this condition. Koozh is extremely hydrating, cooling, and electrolyte-rich from the fermentation and salt.
Highly beneficial — live Lactobacillus colonize gut and reduce inflammation. Start with small amounts and increase gradually.
Fermented millets have increased iron bioavailability due to reduced phytates. Excellent for anemia with appropriate iron accompaniments.

10.ayurvedic_wisdom: Koozh is arguably the most sophisticated functional food in South Indian culinary tradition. Tamil Sangam literature from 2000 years ago describes it as the food of kings and warriors — its ability to sustain intense physical labor in summer heat while cooling the body speaks to its Brimhana + Sheeta dual action. Modern microbiology confirms what ancient Tamil farmers knew: the mud pot fermentation creates a unique probiotic ecosystem that no industrial process can replicate.

11.name: Lemon Rice

dosha_effect.summary: Pitta aggravating (Amla rasa + Ushna virya); Vata acceptable; Kapha beneficial (Laghu, cleansing)

ayurvedic_profile.agni_impact: Deepana — mustard seeds + lemon Amla + turmeric create a potent Agni-stimulating combination

nutritional_context.best_meal_time: Lunch only — Pitta dominates at midday; Amla food at dinner aggravates nighttime Pitta

avoid_seasons: Sharad (Sep–Oct) — Pitta is at its annual peak; adding Amla-Ushna food provokes disorders
Grishma (May–Jun) — summer heat already elevates Pitta
ritucharya.reason: The Amla rasa (sour taste) specifically aggravates Pitta. In Pitta-dominant seasons, even a small quantity of sour food can tip balanced Pitta into disorder — skin eruptions, gastritis, inflammation.

prakriti_advice.vata: Good — Amla rasa is one of Vata's pacifying tastes. The mustard seeds and warming spices additionally ground Vata. Acceptable daily in moderate amounts.
prakriti_advice.pitta: Limit to once a week. Always pair with cooling Thengai Chutney. Avoid pairing with other sour items. Never at dinner.
prakriti_advice.kapha: Beneficial — the Laghu, Deepana, Ruksha quality of Lemon Rice is excellent for clearing Kapha accumulation.

home.question: Did you use freshly squeezed lemon juice (not bottled/concentrate), and did you add turmeric with a ghee tadka containing mustard seeds and curry leaves?
home.positive_label: Fresh Lemon + Turmeric + Ghee Tadka
home.negative_label: Bottled Juice + Refined Oil or No Turmeric
home.positive_reasoning: Fresh lemon juice contains active Vitamin C (destroyed in 15 minutes after juicing and never survives bottling), bioflavonoids, and natural citric acid — these are Pitta-moderating when fresh. Turmeric (Haridra) is specifically curcumin, which requires piperine (pepper) or fat (ghee) for absorption — ghee + turmeric is the classical Ayurvedic delivery system. Ghee + mustard seeds create the tridoshic tadka that carries fat-soluble compounds. This combination makes Lemon Rice Deepana without being excessively Pitta-aggravating.
home.negative_reasoning: Bottled lemon juice/concentrate is heated during pasteurization — Vitamin C is fully destroyed, natural bioflavonoids are degraded, and sodium citrate (preservative) is added. This is Artificial Amla — it has the sour taste but none of the therapeutic value, only the Pitta-aggravating property. Refined oil in tadka cannot carry curcumin effectively — the primary anti-inflammatory compound of turmeric goes to waste.

hotel.question: Is the rice very yellow (artificial turmeric color/dye) and excessively oily, or is it a natural light yellow with moderate seasoning?
hotel.positive_label: Natural Light Yellow, Moderate Seasoning
hotel.negative_label: Neon/Deep Yellow, Excessive Oil
hotel.positive_reasoning: Natural turmeric produces a light, muted yellow. Fresh lemon adds brightness. Moderate oil means Laghu guna is preserved. This is Lemon Rice at its best therapeutic value — Deepana, digestive, appropriate for a light lunch accompaniment.
hotel.negative_reasoning: Deep yellow/neon color = artificial food coloring (usually Tartrazine — a Pitta-Rakta toxin). Excessive oil converts Laghu Lemon Rice into Guru — defeating its Kapha-clearing, light nature. Restaurant Lemon Rice is often made from day-old rice (Purva Anna) which makes it Tamasic.
red_flags: Neon/fluorescent yellow color (Tartrazine artificial dye)
Very oily and glistening
Stale or fermented smell (day-old rice reheated)
Excessive sourness (bottled juice used in large quantity)

ideal_with: Thengai Chutney (28) — Sheeta, Madhura offsets the Ushna, Amla
Papad (Vathal/Papadum) — Laghu, crunchy, digestive
A small amount of Sambar (25)
avoid_with: Paal Kolukattai (17) — Sour + Milk = critical Viruddha (curdles in gut)
Any Pitta-aggravating foods in same meal — Pitta stacking
Boiled Egg (2) — Ushna + Ushna with heavy protein

condition_warnings.advice: Contraindicated — Amla rasa directly triggers acid reflux and gastritis. Eliminate during flare-ups.
Lemon citrate actually helps PREVENT new stone formation. However, existing stone patients should discuss with Vaidya.
Beneficial — Vitamin C from fresh lemon dramatically increases non-heme iron absorption. Eat with iron-rich accompaniments.

11.ayurvedic_wisdom: Lemon Rice demonstrates the Ayurvedic principle that even a Pitta-aggravating food can be made therapeutic with proper Samskara (preparation). Fresh lemon, turmeric, ghee, and mustard seeds transform sour rice from a Pitta-provoker into a digestive, iron-absorption-enhancing, mildly Deepana food. The same dish with bottled juice and refined oil is just sour rice with none of the benefits.

12.name: Medu Vadai

dosha_effect.summary: Kapha aggravating; Pitta aggravating (from frying); Vata acceptable in moderation

ayurvedic_profile.agni_impact: Manda (suppresses Agni) — the large amount of oil absorbed during frying creates a post-digestive Agni-dampening effect. The spices initially appear Deepana but the fat wins.

nutritional_context.best_meal_time: Breakfast only — strong morning Agni. Never at dinner. 1–2 maximum.

ideal_seasons: Shishira (Winter) — maximum Agni can handle the heavy food
avoid_seasons: Varsha (Monsoon) — Agni is at its seasonal low; deep-fried food in monsoon is Ama-forming
Grishma (Summer) — excess Ushna
ritucharya.reason: Deep-fried foods require strong Agni to process without creating Ama. Only in winter does the average person have sufficient Agni for safe occasional consumption.

prakriti_advice.vata: Acceptable occasionally — the heavy, warm, slightly oily nature grounds Vata. Do not exceed 2 per meal. Eat warm.
prakriti_advice.pitta: Limit — Ushna + Teekshna from frying aggravates Pitta. If eating, pair with cooling Thengai Chutney only.
prakriti_advice.kapha: Minimize or avoid — Guru + Snigdha is the worst combination for Kapha. If Kapha is already imbalanced (heaviness, mucus), strictly avoid.

home.question: Was it fried in fresh groundnut or coconut oil at correct temperature (170–180°C — a small batter piece should rise and brown in 3–4 seconds)?
home.positive_label: Fresh Oil, Correct Temperature, Crispy Outside
home.negative_label: Reused Oil, Wrong Temperature, Soggy
home.positive_reasoning: Fresh groundnut oil has high smoke point (230°C), good oxidative stability, and high oleic acid content — it creates a quick, crispy crust (less oil absorption) at 170-180°C. Correct temperature means the Vadai surface sets immediately, creating a barrier that reduces total oil absorption by 40-50% vs low-temperature frying. The urad dal proteins denatured properly create a digestible protein matrix.
home.negative_reasoning: Reused oil contains polymerized fat chains, trans fats, and aldehydes — every reheating cycle exponentially increases Visha load. Low-temperature frying allows oil to soak in fully — creating an oil-saturated, Guru-Snigdha bomb that taxes Agni for hours. This is the primary Ama-creating mechanism in fried foods.

hotel.question: When you break it open, does the interior look fluffy and light (properly fried), or dense, soggy, and oil-saturated?
hotel.positive_label: Crispy Outside, Fluffy and Light Inside
hotel.negative_label: Soggy, Oil-Soaked, Dense Interior
hotel.positive_reasoning: Crispy outside, fluffy inside means the Vadai was fried at the correct temperature in oil of reasonable quality. The air pockets inside indicate good batter fermentation and correct frying. This maximizes Laghu quality while preserving protein structure.
hotel.negative_reasoning: Soggy, oil-soaked interior = low-temperature frying in reused oil. The Vadai has absorbed maximum possible oil — converting what should be a protein-rich snack into a vehicle for Dushta Sneha (degraded oil). Dense interior = under-fermented batter or wrong urad dal ratio. This is maximum Guru, maximum Ama-risk.
red_flags: Dark brown/black color (over-fried in too-hot oil — acrolein, acrylamide)
Very oily — leaves significant oil on fingers and plate
Soggy or wet interior (undercooked or low-temperature reused oil)
Rancid or fishy oil smell (reused oil with seafood cross-contamination)

ideal_with: Sambar (25) — the Deepana spices in Sambar help process the heavy Vadai
Thengai Chutney (28) — cooling, digestive
Small amount of Kaara Chutney (8) — Teekshna cuts Kapha from frying
avoid_with: Mutton Biryani (14) — double Guru in one meal, impossible for average Agni
Chicken 65 (4) — double deep-fried heavy protein overload
Cold milk — Guru + Guru = guaranteed Ama

condition_warnings.advice: High calorie density. One Vadai absorbs significant oil. Avoid or limit strictly to 1 per week.
Fried food directly contributes to Medas (fat tissue) accumulation. Minimize.
Urad dal in excess can affect thyroid function (goitrogen). Occasional consumption is fine but daily Medu Vadai is ill-advised.

12.ayurvedic_wisdom: Medu Vadai is beloved precisely because it is occasionally indulgent — Ayurveda never asked for dietary perfection, only dietary wisdom. The key Ayurvedic lesson here is Matra (dose) and Kala (timing): two Vadais at breakfast in winter, once a week, fried in fresh oil, eaten with digestive accompaniments = occasional pleasure without accumulating Ama. The same Vadais from a street stall at 9 PM in summer every day = Kapha-Pitta disorder in 6 months.

13.name: Mushroom Biryani
13.classification: Rice with Mushrooms — Kapha Aggravating (Tamasic ingredient)

dosha_effect.summary: Kapha increasing; Pitta-Vata neutral when well-spiced

ayurvedic_profile.agni_impact: Manda — mushrooms have high water content that dilutes Agni when consumed in large quantities. The aromatic spices partially compensate.

nutritional_context.best_meal_time: Lunch only — heavy grain+mushroom combination needs strong Agni

avoid_seasons: Varsha (Monsoon) — mushrooms grow in damp conditions; monsoon mushrooms carry elevated Kapha and potential toxins. Double Kapha = maximum Ama risk.
ritucharya.reason: Classical Ayurveda classifies mushrooms (Chataka) as Tamasic — they grow in darkness and damp without sunlight (Tejas/fire element). Consuming them in monsoon when atmospheric Kapha is highest compounds this nature.

prakriti_advice.vata: Acceptable with heavy spicing. Mushrooms' Guru-Mridu quality grounds Vata but the Kapha-increasing tendency should be monitored.
prakriti_advice.pitta: Neutral to good — not Ushna like meat biryanis. Keep spice moderate.
prakriti_advice.kapha: Minimize — both mushrooms and rice increase Kapha. If eating, add maximum spice (pepper, cinnamon, cloves) to counter.

home.question: Are the mushrooms fresh (bought same day, no slime, firm texture) and were they sautéed separately to remove excess moisture before adding to rice?
home.positive_label: Fresh Same-Day Mushrooms + Pre-Sautéed
home.negative_label: Canned/Preserved Mushrooms or Not Pre-Sautéed
home.positive_reasoning: Fresh mushrooms have maximum Prana content. Pre-sautéing is the critical step — mushrooms contain 90% water; evaporating this moisture before adding to rice prevents steam-softening the rice and reduces the Kapha-increasing water content dramatically. Pre-sautéed mushrooms develop Maillard compounds (umami) that are actually more bioavailable and better for Agni than raw mushrooms. This reduces Ama risk significantly.
home.negative_reasoning: Canned mushrooms are preserved in brine (sodium-loaded), pasteurized (no living enzymes), and the texture becomes Mridu-Snigdha (soft and wet) — maximum Kapha-increasing form. Not pre-sautéing means all the water releases into the biryani, making it Guru and Sheeta simultaneously — dampening Agni and creating ideal Ama conditions.

hotel.question: Does the biryani smell strongly of artificial masala/MSG, or does the mushroom flavor come through distinctly?
hotel.positive_label: Balanced — Mushroom Flavor Identifiable
hotel.negative_label: Heavy Artificial Masala Overwhelms, MSG Signs
hotel.positive_reasoning: When mushroom flavor is identifiable, the biryani has reasonable ingredient integrity — real mushrooms in appropriate quantity, balanced spicing. This allows the earthy, grounding Guru quality of mushrooms to be therapeutic for Vata.
hotel.negative_reasoning: Heavy masala masking the primary ingredient = over-spiced commercial product hiding poor ingredient quality (old mushrooms, canned mushrooms, minimal actual mushrooms). MSG addition is common. The result is a Visha-Ama combination where the masala creates artificial Deepana stimulus while the poor ingredients accumulate Ama.
red_flags: Rubbery, uniformly sized mushrooms (canned)
Unnaturally yellow rice
Excessive thirst after eating (MSG)
Very oily with oil pooling at the base

ideal_with: Puthina Chutney (24) — digestive, cuts Kapha
Plain Raita with cumin — probiotic support for heavy grain meal
avoid_with: Medu Vadai (12) — double Kapha overload
Any dairy in same meal — mushroom + milk is a subtle Viruddha combination

condition_warnings.advice: Kapha-increasing food. Avoid during active respiratory illness or Kapha accumulation.
Mushrooms are moderate in purines. Limit consumption if uric acid is elevated.
Culinary mushrooms contain beta-glucans (immunomodulating). In this context, moderate consumption is beneficial.

13.ayurvedic_wisdom: Mushrooms sit in an interesting place in Ayurvedic classification — Tamasic by nature (grown without sunlight), yet containing valuable compounds (beta-glucans, ergothioneine) now confirmed by modern research. The Ayurvedic resolution is through Samskara: proper preparation (pre-sautéing to remove excess water, heavy spicing with Deepana spices) transforms a Tamasic ingredient into a more acceptable Rajasic food. Never the ideal choice, but manageable when prepared correctly.

14.name: Mutton Biryani
14.classification: Rice with Mutton — Heavy Rajasic Feast Food

dosha_effect.summary: Kapha-Pitta aggravating; beneficial for Vata when prepared correctly with digestive spices

ayurvedic_profile.vipaka: Guru (Heavy — Madhura with heavy post-digestion load)
ayurvedic_profile.agni_impact: Requires maximum Agni (Tikshna Agni) to digest without Ama. The whole spices are Deepana but the heavy fat and protein dominate post-digestively.

nutritional_context.best_meal_time: Lunch only — earliest possible (11:30 AM–1 PM). Never at dinner. Never on empty stomach.

ideal_seasons: Hemanta (Nov–Dec) — Agni is at maximum strength
avoid_seasons: Grishma (May–Jun)
Sharad (Sep–Oct) — Pitta is elevated; adding Ushna-Guru mutton creates inflammatory cascade
ritucharya.reason: Mutton is the heaviest meat in Ayurvedic classification. Only the body's winter-maximum Agni can process it without accumulating Ama in the Mamsa and Meda dhatus.

prakriti_advice.vata: Best meat for Vata — heavy, warm, nourishing, and specifically Brimhana for Vata's depleted Dhatus. With proper digestive spices, this is Vata's feast food.
prakriti_advice.pitta: Limit to once a month maximum. Ushna virya of mutton directly aggravates Pitta. Never eat with hot chutneys.
prakriti_advice.kapha: Minimize or avoid — the heaviest food for the heaviest dosha. If eating, small portion with maximum pepper and no dessert after.

home.question: Is the mutton from a good source (ideally grass-fed/free-range), tender-cooked with digestive spices (ginger, pepper, fennel, mint), and made with ghee?
home.positive_label: Good Quality Mutton + Full Digestive Spice Base + Ghee
home.negative_label: Factory-Farmed Tough Mutton + Masala Powder Only + Cheap Oil
home.positive_reasoning: Grass-fed/free-range mutton has 3-4x higher omega-3 fatty acids and lower inflammatory arachidonic acid vs factory-farmed. Ginger + pepper = Mamsala combination specifically recommended by Charaka for heavy meat preparations (increases protease enzyme activity for protein digestion). Fennel + mint = Vata-pacifying aromatic spices that moderate the Ushna virya of meat. Ghee specifically reduces meat's Ushna nature (per Charaka Samhita: Go-Ghrita + Mamsa = Pitta moderation) while building Ojas.
home.negative_reasoning: Factory-farmed mutton carries antibiotic residues, growth hormones, and high omega-6 inflammatory fats — Visha accumulation in Mamsa dhatu. Tough meat = poor quality or old animal = high collagen-to-protein ratio, much harder to digest. Masala powder-only = no fresh volatile compounds from whole spices, excess sodium, often old and rancid. Cheap oil (Dalda/refined) = trans fats in already high-fat meat preparation = maximum Ama.

hotel.question: Is there a visible orange oil/fat layer floating on top of the biryani, and does the meat pull apart easily or remain tough?
hotel.positive_label: Moderate Glistening, Tender Meat that Falls Off Bone
hotel.negative_label: Visible Fat Layer Pooling, Tough Chewy Meat
hotel.positive_reasoning: Tender meat confirms proper slow cooking (Dum method) which breaks down collagen into gelatin — making heavy protein digestible. Moderate glistening (not pooling) means appropriate fat used. Well-cooked biryani has harmonized flavors — rice, spice, and meat are one.
hotel.negative_reasoning: Orange oil pool = Dalda or excess cheap oil — Visha-laden fat. Tough meat = under-cooked or poor-quality old mutton — undigested heavy protein is the worst Ama-forming substance in Ayurvedic food science. The combination of Dalda + tough mutton in restaurant biryani is a maximum Ojas-depleting combination.
red_flags: Visible orange-red oil pool at bottom of container
Tough, chewy mutton pieces (old meat or under-cooked)
Extremely thirsty 30 min after (MSG)
Very heavy, bloated feeling lasting 4+ hours
Unnaturally yellow or orange colored rice

ideal_with: Puthina Chutney (24) — cooling digestive
Plain Raita — probiotics for heavy protein
Fresh lime with salt — digestive Agni support
avoid_with: Paal Kolukattai (17) — Meat + Milk: critical Viruddha Ahara (skin disorders, allergic cascade)
Any cold drinks — suppresses Agni needed to process meat
Medu Vadai (12) or any other fried food in same meal

condition_warnings.advice: High purine content. Contraindicated during flare-ups. Strict limitation even in remission.
Avoid restaurant versions. Home version with good quality meat and ghee, once a month, may be acceptable under Vaidya guidance.
Contraindicated — Ushna virya of mutton + Pitta disorders = inflammatory aggravation.

14.ayurvedic_wisdom: Mutton Biryani is the supreme feast food — designed for warriors, laborers, and occasions. Charaka specifically recommends goat meat (Aja Mamsa) as the best meat for Brimhana (building tissue), particularly for Vata disorders and post-illness recovery. The tragedy is that this potentially therapeutic food has become a vehicle for Visha (reused oil, MSG, artificial color) in most restaurant kitchens. Eaten once a month at home with care, it is a celebration. Eaten weekly from restaurants, it is a silent accumulator of disease.

15.name: Nandu (Crab) Masala
15.classification: Spiced Crab — Heavy Seafood, Pitta Provoking

dosha_effect.summary: Pitta increasing; Kapha increasing; Vata acceptable in small amounts

ayurvedic_profile.agni_impact: Deepana when pepper-based; Manda when over-spiced with excess chili (Teekshna suppresses after initial spike)

nutritional_context.best_meal_time: Lunch only — absolute maximum. Never dinner.

avoid_seasons: Grishma (May–Jun)
Sharad — Pitta seasons. Seafood + Pitta season = skin disorders, inflammation, allergic reactions
ritucharya.reason: Classical Ayurveda notes that seafood has natural Ushna-Tikshna virya. In hot seasons when Pitta is already elevated, this creates Rakta (blood) Pitta conditions — skin eruptions, inflammation, hyperacidity.

prakriti_advice.vata: Small portions only. The weight and warmth ground Vata but the Tikshna quality aggravates in excess.
prakriti_advice.pitta: Limit to once a month maximum. Always with pepper-based (not chili-based) preparation and coconut milk to moderate heat.
prakriti_advice.kapha: Acceptable — Ushna quality helps Kapha. But Guru nature of crab means moderation.

home.question: Is the masala primarily pepper-based (not excess red chili), and did you add coconut milk to cool and balance the preparation?
home.positive_label: Pepper-Based Masala + Coconut Milk Balance
home.negative_label: Excess Red Chili, No Coconut Milk
home.positive_reasoning: Black pepper (Maricha) is specifically recommended by Charaka for seafood preparations — it is Katu-Ushna but Deepana without the Pitta-Rakta aggravation of red chili capsaicin. Pepper's piperine stimulates bile secretion (appropriate for heavy protein digestion) without irritating the gut lining. Coconut milk provides Sheeta-Snigdha counter to the Ushna seafood — this is classical Virya-Viruddhata moderation (using opposite quality to balance).
home.negative_reasoning: Excess red chili (Capsaicin) is Teekshna-Ushna-Tikshna — it directly aggravates Pitta in the Rakta dhatu. Combined with the naturally Ushna crab, this creates Rakta-Pitta conditions: skin eruptions, hyperacidity, inflammation. The masking effect of heavy chili also hides the freshness of the crab — possibly concealing older, less fresh seafood.

hotel.question: Is the crab masala gravy intensely spiced to the point where you cannot taste the crab itself, or can the natural seafood sweetness come through?
hotel.positive_label: Balanced — Natural Crab Sweetness Identifiable
hotel.negative_label: Overwhelmingly Spiced — Masking Freshness
hotel.positive_reasoning: Fresh crab has a natural sweet, oceanic flavor (Madhura rasa). When this comes through the spices, it confirms fresh, quality seafood with balanced preparation. Fresh crab has high Omega-3, zinc, and B12 which are genuinely Ojas-building nutrients.
hotel.negative_reasoning: Over-spicing to the point of masking the primary ingredient is a classic sign of concealing non-fresh seafood. Old or not-fresh crab undergoes rapid protein breakdown (histamine production) — this histamine creates direct Pitta-Rakta aggravation independent of the spices. This is the most dangerous form of Visha in seafood.
red_flags: Overwhelming spice that makes crab undetectable (freshness masking)
Slightly ammonia-like smell (protein degradation — non-fresh)
Excessively red and oily gravy
Rubbery, overcooked crab meat (overcooking to kill bacteria in questionable freshness)

ideal_with: White Rice (27) — neutral Madhura-vipaka rice moderates the Katu-Ushna crab
Thengai Chutney (28) is not ideal here — but coconut rice would work
avoid_with: Paal Kolukattai (17) — Crab + Milk: CRITICAL Viruddha Ahara. Classical Ayurveda identifies this as causing skin toxicity and vitiligo.
Any dairy in same meal — Viruddha Ahara principle
Cold drinks — suppresses Agni for heavy protein

condition_warnings.advice: High risk of allergic reaction. Cross-reactivity between crab and other shellfish is common.
Crab is moderate-high in purines. Contraindicated during flare-ups.
Seafood is specifically identified in Ayurveda as Rakta-Pitta aggravating. Avoid entirely during skin disorder treatment.

15.ayurvedic_wisdom: Crab is a Viruddha Ahara landmine — its incompatibility with milk and dairy is one of the most firmly established food incompatibilities in classical Ayurveda. Beyond this, crab's therapeutic value (zinc, B12, selenium for thyroid health) is real, but its Ushna virya means it is medicine in the right season, right preparation, and right quantity — and a Pitta-Rakta disorder in the wrong context.

16.name: Nei Satham (Ghee Rice)
16.classification: Pure Cow Ghee Rice — Supreme Ojas-Building Food

dosha_effect.summary: Vata-Pitta pacifying; Kapha neutral (ghee's intelligence doesn't increase Kapha pathologically)

ayurvedic_profile.virya: Sheeta (Cooling — cow ghee is specifically Sheeta despite being fat)
guna: Laghu (Surprisingly light — cow ghee is Laghu despite appearance)
ayurvedic_profile.agni_impact: Brimhana-Deepana: Cow ghee is the only fat that simultaneously nourishes and kindles Agni. Small amounts are Deepana; generous amounts are Brimhana.

nutritional_context.best_meal_time: Lunch — primary midday meal with accompaniments
nutritional_context.digestibility: easy to moderate (ghee is surprisingly easy on Agni)

ideal_seasons: Grishma (Summer) — Sheeta virya of ghee is particularly valuable
Varsha (Monsoon) — Brimhana when Agni is weak
avoid_seasons: No complete avoidance. Reduce quantity in Kapha seasons (Hemanta/Shishira). Counter with pepper and cumin.
ritucharya.reason: Cow ghee is one of the few foods with no seasonal contraindication in classical Ayurveda — it is used in Panchakarma preparations year-round precisely because it is safe across all seasons.

prakriti_advice.vata: Excellent — the single best food for Vata. Generous ghee, warm rice, grounding. Charaka places cow ghee + rice as the primary Vata-pacifying meal.
prakriti_advice.pitta: Excellent — Sheeta virya directly cools Pitta. The only fat that doesn't aggravate Pitta. More ghee = more Pitta pacification (within reason).
prakriti_advice.kapha: Acceptable in moderate amounts — 1 tablespoon ghee per serving. Ghee's Laghu quality (despite being fat) doesn't increase Kapha pathologically. Pair with pepper and cumin.

home.question: Is it made with pure cow ghee (golden color, distinct nutty aroma, clarified at home or from a trusted A2 source)?
home.positive_label: Pure Cow Ghee — Golden, Nutty, Aromatic
home.negative_label: Dalda / Vegetable Fat / Buffalo Ghee / Mixed Fat
home.positive_reasoning: Pure cow ghee (Go-Ghrita, especially A2 cow varieties like Gir, Sahiwal) is the single highest Ojas-building food in the Ayurvedic pharmacopeia. It contains: butyric acid (heals gut lining, reduces inflammation), CLA (anti-cancer, body composition), fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, K2, and short-chain fatty acids that cross the blood-brain barrier — nourishing Majja dhatu (nervous system). Charaka Samhita states: 'Of all fats, Go-Ghrita is the best. It promotes Ojas, Medha (intellect), Agni, Strength, and Lifespan.' This is the highest praise given to any single food in classical Ayurveda.
home.negative_reasoning: Dalda (Vanaspati) contains 30-40% trans fats — the most pathological dietary fat known. Trans fats incorporate into cell membranes, disrupting all cellular signaling. They specifically reduce Tejas (metabolic intelligence) at the cellular level — the exact opposite of ghee's action. Buffalo ghee is heavier, more Kapha-increasing, and lacks the specific Rasayana compounds of cow ghee. The golden color and nutty aroma of real ghee cannot be faked — these indicate milk solid browning (Maillard products) that are uniquely bioavailable.

hotel.question: Does the rice have a waxy, artificial sheen and bland fatty smell (Dalda), or a golden, aromatic, nutty fragrance (real ghee)?
hotel.positive_label: Golden, Aromatic, Nutty Ghee Fragrance
hotel.negative_label: Waxy Sheen, Bland or Artificial Fat Smell
hotel.positive_reasoning: Real cow ghee is unmistakable — the nutty aroma from browned milk solids (diacetyl compounds) cannot be replicated by any other fat. If the hotel uses real ghee, Nei Satham is one of the most Ojas-building meals you can eat outside your home.
hotel.negative_reasoning: A waxy sheen when the rice cools (like candle wax) is the diagnostic sign of hydrogenated vegetable fat (Dalda). The bland, slightly chemical smell of refined vegetable oil is another indicator.
